content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Turning a 90-minute podcast into ten shorts isn’t a prompt problem. It’s a pipeline problem — and the difference matters for anything you build with AI.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every few days someone shows me the same screenshot. They’ve pasted a YouTube link into ChatGPT and asked it to “make 10 viral shorts from this video.” The reply is always confident, always well-formatted, and always fiction. It describes clips that don’t exist, timestamps it invented, and captions for words nobody said.
This isn’t a failure of the model. It’s a failure of shape. We’ve been handed an astonishing general-purpose text engine, and our instinct is to throw every problem at it as a single prompt. But some problems aren’t text problems wearing a costume. Video repurposing is one of them, and understanding why teaches you something that applies to almost every serious AI product: the model is one component, not the system.
Let me show you what’s actually under the hood of “make me a short,” because once you see it, you can’t unsee it.
What “make a short” actually means
Take a real task: you have a two-hour interview and you want ten vertical clips for TikTok and Shorts. Here’s the work, honestly decomposed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Now look at that list and ask: how many of these steps are text problems?
Exactly one and a half. Step 6 is genuinely a language task — that’s where an LLM shines. Step 3 is partly language (you can reason over a transcript). Everything else is pixels, waveforms, timestamps, and codecs. A language model has no native access to any of it.
The fundamental mismatch: tokens vs. samples
Here’s the core idea worth internalizing, because it generalizes far beyond video.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A large language model operates on tokens — chunks of text — and produces the statistically likely next token. That’s the whole game. It’s extraordinary at it. But a video is not tokens. A video is a grid of pixels sampled over time, plus an audio waveform sampled tens of thousands of times per second. When you ask ChatGPT for a clip “from 4:12 to 4:48,” it isn’t measuring anything. There’s no clock, no decoder, no frame buffer. It’s generating text that looks like an answer because, in its training data, answers to such questions looked like that.
Three properties of real editing make this gap unbridgeable by prompting alone:
Determinism. A cut at 4:12.300 must be that cut, every time, exactly. LLMs are probabilistic by design — the same prompt can give different answers. You do not want “probably the right frame.”
Frame accuracy. Captions that drift 200ms look broken. A cut that lands mid-syllable feels amateur. This is arithmetic on a timeline, and you want a tool that does arithmetic, not one that does plausible-sounding arithmetic.
Duration. Rendering a single short is seconds to minutes of real CPU/GPU work. A model’s reply is one short burst of token generation. These are different physical activities. You can’t render a video “inside” a chat response any more than you can bake a cake inside a text message describing one.
So the question isn’t “which model is smart enough?” The question is: what’s the right tool for each step, and how do you wire them together?
What you actually build instead
When you stop treating the LLM as the application, the real architecture appears. It’s a pipeline: a sequence of specialized stages, each doing the one thing it’s good at, passing structured data to the next.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Notice the LLM shows up twice — at segmentation and rewriting — and nowhere else. The rest is purpose-built media tooling: yt-dlp for download, Whisper for word-timed transcription, FFmpeg for cutting and assembly, a vision pass for framing, a TTS engine for voice. Each is better at its job than any general model will ever be, because each was built to do exactly that and nothing else.
This is the first fundamental: composition beats generality. The valuable system isn’t the smartest single model; it’s the correct orchestration of narrow tools.
The LLM belongs behind an interface
Here’s the second fundamental, and the one most “AI app” projects get wrong. Where you do use the model — finding segments, rewriting scripts — you should hide it behind a contract, not call it directly.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why bother? Because “the LLM” is not one thing. Today it’s one vendor’s model; tomorrow it’s a cheaper one, a local one, or three of them with fallbacks. The same is true of transcription and voice. If your pipeline calls a specific API directly, you’ve welded your product to a vendor’s pricing and uptime. If it calls an adapter, swapping the engine is a config change.
The lesson here is brutal and worth saying plainly: “we call a powerful model” is not a moat. Anyone can call the same model. The durable value is everywhere around the model — the segmentation logic tuned to a specific channel, the caption styles that perform, the approval data that teaches the system what “good” looks like for you. The model is rented. The workflow is owned.
The unglamorous backbone: durable jobs
Here’s the part nobody tweets about, and the part that actually makes this a product instead of a script.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Each of those pipeline stages takes real time and can fail — a download stalls, a render runs out of memory, a provider times out. If you run them inline in a web request, the first crash loses everything and your user stares at a spinner for ten minutes. So you don’t. You put a queue in front of the work and run it in background workers, and you make every stage two things:

• Resumable — if transcription succeeded but the render died, you restart from the render, not from the download.

• Idempotent — running a stage twice produces the same result, so a retry is always safe.
This is the third fundamental, and it’s true of every non-trivial AI system, not just video: the model is the easy part; the orchestration is the product. Reliability, resumability, cost control, and observability are what separate a demo from something people pay for. A chat box has none of this. It can’t, by design — it’s stateless and synchronous, which is perfect for conversation and wrong for a 12-minute, eight-stage job.
Where humans stay in the loop
There’s a final reason a prompt can’t own this workflow: you’re putting your name on the output.
A fully automated “10 viral clips, zero clicks” tool sounds great until it confidently mis-cuts your best moment or captions a word you didn’t say. The right design treats the AI as a fast first draft and the human as the editor: adjust the crop, nudge a split point, fix a line, approve. The metric that matters isn’t “clips generated” — it’s approved clips per week. Generation is cheap. Publishable is the whole game, and publishable runs through a person.
This reframes the entire goal. You’re not automating the human away. You’re making one person feel like a full editing team — which is a much better business than pretending the team isn’t needed.
So I built the pipeline, not the prompt
I got tired of explaining all of this in DMs, so I built it. VolfPack (volfpack.online) takes a long-form video you own and runs it through exactly the pipeline above — download, frame-accurate transcription, LLM-assisted segmenting and rewriting behind adapters, vertical reframing, caption timing, FFmpeg assembly — with durable jobs underneath and human review editors on top. You paste a source, watch each phase run, fix anything the AI got wrong, and export. The LLM is in there. It’s just doing its actual job — language — instead of being asked to hallucinate a video editor.
You can try it on a video of your own at volfpack.online. The point I’d love you to take away isn’t “use my thing,” though — it’s the mental model. Next time you’re tempted to solve a problem with one heroic prompt, decompose it the way we decomposed “make a short.” Ask which steps are really text, which need a deterministic tool, and what has to survive a crash. Usually you’ll find the model is one well-placed component in a system that does the actual work.
The takeaway, stated as a principle
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s a superb function — give it language, get back language, and it’ll outperform anything we’ve built before. But an application is the thing that knows when to call that function, what to do with the other 80% of the problem, how to recover when a step fails, and how to keep a human in control of the output. ChatGPT can’t edit your video for the same reason a calculator can’t file your taxes: it’s a brilliant component aimed at the wrong altitude.
Build the pipeline. Use the model where it earns its place. And put your name only on the things a person approved.
If you want to see the whole pipeline working on your own footage, it’s live at volfpack.online.
